---
title: "Diagnostics"
---

This page contains technical diagnostic figures intended to support model development and peer review. They document the structure of the data, the behavior of residuals, and key modeling choices. Most of these figures won't appear in the final paper but are essential for catching systematic errors — for example, confirming that the choice of data transformation (Box-Cox) is justified, or that errors are not correlated with time in a way that would bias the holdout evaluation.

## Per-Site R2 Distribution (CDF)

A CDF (cumulative distribution function) reads as: "what fraction of holdout sites achieve R² at or below this value?" This view makes it easy to read off the median (50th percentile), the fraction of sites above zero (the model beats predicting the mean), and the long tail of poorly-predicted sites. Three lines are shown: zero-shot (no local samples), N=5, and N=10 adaptation. The curves should shift right with more samples — meaning more sites cross into positive R² territory. The vertical line at R²=0 is the critical threshold: sites to the left represent complete model failures at those locations.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-r2-cdf
from pathlib import Path
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, ".")
from _scripts.figures import make_per_site_r2_cdf

fig = make_per_site_r2_cdf(Path("dashboard_data/per_site.parquet"))
fig.show()
```

## Residual Diagnostics

A residual is the difference between a predicted value and the true observed value. Ideally, residuals should be random noise with no pattern — if they fan out at high predicted values (heteroscedasticity), cluster around certain turbidity levels (input dependence), or show heavy tails (extreme errors more common than a normal distribution would predict), those patterns point to model mis-specification. This four-panel figure is the standard diagnostic battery. The heavy-tailed residual distribution shown here directly motivated using a Student-t prior in the Bayesian adaptation rather than a Gaussian, because the Student-t is more robust to extreme, outlier-inducing observations.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-residual-diagnostics
from _scripts.figures import make_residual_diagnostics

fig = make_residual_diagnostics(Path("dashboard_data/per_reading.parquet"))
fig.show()
```

## Temporal Stability

A model that performs well in the early part of a site's record but poorly in the later part (or vice versa) may be exploiting temporal coincidences rather than physical relationships. Sensor drift, land use change, wildfire, or channel migration can all shift the turbidity-SSC relationship over time. This figure splits each site's data chronologically and compares R² from the first half of the record against R² from the second half. Sites near the diagonal are stable. Sites far from it warrant further investigation — and may indicate that more frequent sensor calibration is needed in the field.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-temporal-stability
from _scripts.figures import make_temporal_stability

fig = make_temporal_stability(Path("dashboard_data/per_reading.parquet"))
fig.show()
```

## Data Funnel

Building a cross-site dataset requires strict quality filters at every step — and each filter removes sites, reducing the final sample. This Sankey diagram traces that attrition: starting from 860 candidate USGS gauges that have both turbidity and SSC sensors, sites are progressively excluded for lacking overlapping records, insufficient sample counts, missing watershed attributes (land cover, geology), or falling into the reserved vault set. Understanding how many sites were lost at each step — and why — is critical for assessing whether the final 260 training sites are representative of US streams in general.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-data-sankey
from _scripts.figures import make_data_sankey

fig = make_data_sankey(Path("dashboard_data/summary.json"))
fig.show()
```

## SSC vs Turbidity Joint Distribution

This is the fundamental empirical relationship the model is learning: how does turbidity (the sensor reading, x-axis) relate to SSC (the lab-measured truth, y-axis) across thousands of paired observations from 78 holdout sites? A tight, consistent relationship would be easy to model; the scatter seen here reflects genuine site-to-site variation in sediment grain size, organic content, and sensor calibration. Dense color regions show the most common combinations; sparse scatter at the edges shows storm-event extremes. Features that shift a site's relationship away from the center cloud are exactly what watershed attributes (geology, slope, land cover) are intended to capture.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-joint-distribution
from _scripts.figures import make_joint_distribution

fig = make_joint_distribution(Path("dashboard_data/per_reading.parquet"))
fig.show()
```

## Box-Cox Transform Effect

SSC values are highly right-skewed: most readings are low, but a handful of extreme storm events produce values 100× larger. Training a model on raw SSC values would make it almost entirely focused on predicting those rare extremes, ignoring the majority of observations. Applying a mathematical transformation before training compresses the scale so that errors at low SSC count alongside errors at high SSC. The most common choice is a log transform; here a **Box-Cox transform with lambda=0.2** is used instead. This figure shows the SSC distribution under three candidate transforms (raw, log, Box-Cox) to justify that choice: Box-Cox reduces skewness substantially while preserving more of the high-SSC structure than a full log transform would.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-boxcox-comparison
from _scripts.figures import make_boxcox_comparison

fig = make_boxcox_comparison(Path("dashboard_data/per_reading.parquet"))
fig.show()
```

## Power-Law Slope Distribution

When turbidity and SSC are both plotted on log scales (log-log space), the relationship at each site approximates a straight line — a power law. The slope of that line carries physical meaning: a slope of exactly 1.0 means a doubling of turbidity produces a doubling of SSC (proportional, linear). A slope less than 1 means SSC grows more slowly than turbidity, which typically indicates coarse-grained sediment where particles saturate the sensor at high loads. A slope greater than 1 means SSC accelerates faster than turbidity, characteristic of fine-grained (silty/clayey) watersheds where particles stay in suspension easily. This distribution of slopes across all sites reveals whether a single global slope assumption would be reasonable — it is not — and why site-specific adaptation is necessary.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-slope-gallery
from _scripts.figures import make_slope_gallery

fig = make_slope_gallery(Path("dashboard_data/per_reading.parquet"))
fig.show()
```

## Rating Curves

A **rating curve** is the calibrated relationship between turbidity (the sensor signal) and SSC (the quantity we want to know) at a specific site. Traditionally, rating curves are fit individually at each site using local grab samples. This figure shows six holdout sites selected to span the full range of model performance — from excellent to poor. Viewing the raw data alongside model predictions reveals whether failures are due to noisy data, a fundamentally different relationship at that site, or gaps in the specific concentration range the model is missing. Gray dots are observed paired measurements; colored dots show where the model predicted those same turbidity readings to fall.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-rating-curves
from _scripts.figures import make_rating_curves

fig = make_rating_curves(
    Path("dashboard_data/per_reading.parquet"),
    Path("dashboard_data/per_site.parquet"),
)
fig.show()
```

## Feature Importance

The model uses 72 input features: the turbidity reading itself, plus watershed characteristics such as land cover fractions, soil texture, geology, drainage area, and long-term climate averages. To understand which features matter and *how* they matter, we use SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) — a method that computes each feature's contribution to each individual prediction.

### Beeswarm Plot

Each dot in this plot represents one of the 6,026 holdout observations. Dots are spread horizontally by how much that feature pushed the prediction up (right) or down (left) for that particular observation. Features are ranked vertically — the most influential at the top. Dot color shows the raw feature value: blue means the feature was low for that observation, red means it was high. This lets you read *direction*: for example, if the `turbidity_instant` row shows red dots clustered to the right, that means high turbidity pushes predictions upward, which is physically expected. A feature where blue and red dots are intermixed without clear direction contributes to predictions but in a context-dependent way (its effect depends on other features).

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-feature-importance
from _scripts.figures import make_shap_beeswarm

fig = make_shap_beeswarm(
    shap_path=Path("dashboard_data/deep_holdout_shap_values.parquet"),
    feature_values_path=Path("dashboard_data/deep_holdout_feature_values.parquet"),
    top_n=137,
)
fig.show()
```

### Bar Chart View

The same features, ranked by average importance (mean |SHAP value|), colored by category. This view is simpler to read for comparing magnitudes — which features matter most overall — but doesn't show direction. The beeswarm above tells you *how* each feature influences predictions; the bars below tell you *how much*.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-feature-importance-bars
from _scripts.figures import make_shap_bar_chart

fig = make_shap_bar_chart(Path("dashboard_data/shap_importance.parquet"))
fig.show()
```

## Feature Importance by Regime

The overall feature importance tells you what matters *on average* — but the model may rely on completely different features during a low-flow baseflow period versus a major storm event. This section slices the full 6,026-sample holdout SHAP data by turbidity regime and by collection method to reveal those shifts.

### By Turbidity Regime

Each row is a feature; each column is a turbidity range (low to extreme). Color intensity shows how important that feature is *relative to its own maximum across regimes* — so a bright cell means "this feature matters most in this regime." Look for features that light up only at the extremes (storm-specific drivers) or only at low turbidity (baseflow confounders). If turbidity_instant dominates uniformly across all regimes, the model isn't doing much beyond a simple rating curve; if watershed features light up at high turbidity, the model is genuinely using site context when it matters most.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-shap-regime-heatmap
from _scripts.figures import make_shap_regime_heatmap

fig = make_shap_regime_heatmap(
    Path("dashboard_data/deep_holdout_shap_values.parquet"),
    Path("dashboard_data/deep_holdout_feature_values.parquet"),
    top_n=25,
)
fig.show()
```

### By Collection Method

Different sampling methods introduce different biases. Depth-integrated samples capture the full water column and are considered the gold standard; auto-point samplers grab water at a fixed depth (often near the bank); manual grab samples are the least consistent. If the model leans heavily on `collection_method` for one group but not another, it's compensating for a systematic measurement difference rather than learning hydrology. This figure shows whether the model's feature usage shifts depending on how the sample was collected.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-shap-by-method
from _scripts.figures import make_shap_regime_by_group

fig = make_shap_regime_by_group(
    Path("dashboard_data/deep_holdout_shap_values.parquet"),
    Path("dashboard_data/deep_holdout_feature_values.parquet"),
    group_col="collection_method",
    top_n=15,
)
fig.show()
```

### By Geology Class

The dominant bedrock type controls sediment grain size, mineral composition, and how turbidity relates to SSC. A model that "knows" geology should adjust its feature usage depending on the watershed type — for example, relying more on hydrograph features in flashy shale watersheds and more on baseflow chemistry in carbonate terrain. This figure shows whether that pattern exists.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-shap-by-geology
from _scripts.figures import make_shap_regime_by_group

fig = make_shap_regime_by_group(
    Path("dashboard_data/deep_holdout_shap_values.parquet"),
    Path("dashboard_data/deep_holdout_feature_values.parquet"),
    group_col="geol_class",
    top_n=15,
)
fig.show()
```

## Feature Interactions

Two features *interact* when the effect of one depends on the value of the other. For example, turbidity's impact on predicted SSC might be larger in shale watersheds than in granite ones — that's a turbidity × geology interaction. This matrix shows the strength of every pairwise interaction among the top 20 features, computed via SHAP interaction values on 2,000 holdout samples. The diagonal (self-interaction, i.e., the main effect) is zeroed out so that off-diagonal interactions are visible. Strong off-diagonal cells reveal where the model is doing something more sophisticated than additive feature effects — it's learning conditional relationships between features.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-shap-interactions
from _scripts.figures import make_shap_interaction_heatmap

fig = make_shap_interaction_heatmap(
    Path("dashboard_data/deep_interaction_values.parquet"),
    top_n=20,
)
fig.show()
```

## Failure Site Analysis

About 23% of holdout sites have R² < 0, meaning the model is worse than predicting the site's long-run average. What's going wrong at these sites? This figure compares the average feature importance across all 78 holdout sites (blue) against only the 18 failure sites (red). If a feature is much more important at failure sites, the model may be over-relying on it when it shouldn't be. If a feature is much *less* important, the model may be ignoring a signal it needs. Differences here point toward what would need to change — either in the model or in the data — to rescue these sites.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-shap-failure
from _scripts.figures import make_shap_failure_comparison

fig = make_shap_failure_comparison(
    Path("dashboard_data/deep_per_site_shap.parquet"),
    Path("dashboard_data/deep_failure_site_shap.parquet"),
    top_n=20,
)
fig.show()
```
